15 min read
0:00 / 15:24
If you have completed the exercises from Part I, you should have
re�ned lists of your expertise, skills, and experience and have an
initial idea for a minimum viable audience that could bene�t
from what you already know (or can acquire through learning and
practice).
Don’t worry if what you’ve done so far is not perfect. We’re going
to iterate our way to success, and all you need now is a starting
point to take action.
We want to immerse ourselves completely into the needs and
desires of our minimum viable audience and capture how those
needs and desires are expressed by that audience (in their own
words, thinking, and observed behavior).
We’ll compile those insights over time in documents, Empathy
Maps, and Value Proposition Canvases.
It’s important to be clear that we can’t tell you exactly how to use
the list of resources below because we don’t know the way in
which your interests, experience, and abilities intersect with your
intended audience.
You may be leaning toward a physical product, for example, which
Discover, Part II: Research and
Immersion
MP3 File 5.29 MB
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 15 --

would be different than researching services (which is different
than researching digital products, etc.)
We’re going to give you guidelines and resources that you will
need to experiment with and make your own. Again, perfection is
the enemy — progress is the goal.
The common thread among all of the resources we’ll share is
we’re researching our audiences in media where those audiences
communicate their needs and desires as individuals.
For example, Amazon reviews, Facebook Groups, Reddit threads,
Twitter conversations, and Medium article comments give us
opportunities to understand the needs and desires of audiences
we may want to serve.
Each of those examples (and more in our list below) are helpful
because we have access to the opinions of individuals expressed
in their own words. (Tools like Buzzsumo are also useful because
they aggregate data, but that’s not the same as capturing ideas
directly from individuals in their own words.)
One �nal note before we dive in — you do NOT need to explore
every resource in this list. That’s a really effective way to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 15 --

procrastinate, and that’s not our intent.
Instead, we want you to pick the most appropriate resources for
your speci�c audience and then get started. You’ll likely �nd
signi�cant, useful insights from 2-3 sources, which you’ll be able
to con�rm or discon�rm using the Validate Process later.
Being Your Audience vs. Researching
An Audience
From experience, we can say there is a world of difference
between being (one of) your audience and someone just
researching an audience they are not a member of.
Writing a short piece of �ction will reveal more about what you
don’t know than reading �ve books on the craft of writing or just
being a �y on the wall of a writing forum or group. We can’t
overstate how different these two dynamics are.
Thinking about being a programmer is different from writing a
program that does something useful (which will reveal everything
you don’t know). Researching about how to build a racing
simulator is nothing like actually building a racing simulator
(which will reveal nuances you would have never considered).
The point is, being your own customer, solving your own
problems, pains, gains, and jobs to be done, is one of the few little
secrets worth paying attention to.
It sticks out like a steak stand at a Vegan convention when we see
marketers trying to sell something they have zero experience in
(no results of their own or problem they’ve solved themselves).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 15 --

Don’t be that person. Become your own customer of one, then pull
on the threads that emerge and reveal themselves.
Amazon Reviews
Amazon product reviews can be a gold mine of insights about
buying motivations, expectations, and features / bene�ts that
really matter.
To start, �nd products that help you locate the audience you hope
to serve. For example, �nd the top 3-5 books about a topic that
interests your audience, read the reviews, and look for patterns.
▪ What do people complain about?
▪ What do they mention as being important?
▪ Why did they buy the book in the �rst place (what problem
were they trying to solve?)
▪ Where did the author succeed?
▪ Where did the author fall short?
Often you’ll �nd links to other books about that topic too, with
reasons why one book is better (or, occasionally, worse), according
to the reviewer. That’s a very effective way to �nd the most
impactful books for a particular subject (which begins to create a
map of meaningful resources for you to investigate).
To be clear, we’re not suggesting you read book reviews so you can
write a better book. Instead, we know that books are a common
method people use to �nd solutions to problems and explore
meaningful interests.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 15 --

We’re using books, in this example, as a proxy / entry point into
the deeper topics our audience is interested in.
Let’s consider an example.
In 2016, Tim Ferriss published Tools of Titans: The Tactics,
Routines, and Habits of Billionaires, Icons, and World-Class
Performers.
The reviews for Tools of Titans are a treasure trove of insights
about what Tim’s personal-development-oriented audience was
expecting, and (many) speci�c examples explaining where and
how the author didn’t deliver on those expectations.
Here’s one review (on the �rst page) that’s particularly interesting.
It’s also important to notice that 3,600+ people found that review
helpful. That’s a lot of social proof con�rming that the themes
that reviewer mentioned resonate with (many) others.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 15 --

Tools of Titans is an extreme example. Tim Ferriss has an
enormous personal-development-oriented audience and his books
are popular. You’re not going to �nd that in every market and
that’s OK. You’re looking for themes and insights — reviews are
wonderful resources to �nd both.
If you’re considering creating a physical product, use the same
process but focus on products that ful�ll the same needs / meet
the same desires you’re interested in. Mine the reviews to �nd
problems with current solutions, suggestions to improve existing
products, and other valuable information.
When you read reviews, you’re looking for recurring themes, and
you want to capture those themes in the language of your
audience.
2 and 4 star reviews are fertile grounds to mine for themes and
insights where the author (or product) fell short in some
meaningful way.
Facebook and LinkedIn Groups
Facebook Groups and LinkedIn Groups are broadly useful for
audience research.
Early on in the process (the �rst few times through the TDB
�ywheel), we’re just trying to gather data and organize it into
coherent themes to identify a minimum viable audience and their
speci�c jobs, pains, and gains.
Don’t obsess about �nding and joining every relevant group
(you’ll drive yourself crazy with overwhelm quickly). Instead, look
for 3-5 groups that seem like they’re good matches and then get to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 15 --

work reading posts and comments.
Initially, you want to con�rm your group selection is a good
match. Read 20-30 posts (and the comments), and decide if you’ve
found your audience. (If not, move on to another group and repeat
the process.)
You’re looking for the questions people ask, the issues they’re
struggling with, the answers they get in the comments, resources
they’re referred to by others, and anything else that is useful to
help you better understand their needs and desires.
You’ll also want to determine the sophistication level of the
audience. Are they newbies just getting started or seasoned
veterans who are very familiar with the topic? Context matters.
This is important because one of the decisions you’ll need to make
is where your ideal audience is on the spectrum of least to most
sophisticated.
Keep in mind that you need to be an expert to sell to experts, and
it’s much easier to sell mid-level expertise to novices (and there
are many more novices in markets too).
However, your situation will be unique — just keep audience
sophistication in mind when you’re evaluating posts and
comments.
Reddit Threads and Forums
Reddit threads and online forums can be useful audience research
tools.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 15 --

Start by searching Reddit for threads using broad keyword
phrases, then narrow your search as you begin to uncover the
Reddit landscape for that topic.
To �nd forums, search topic + forum on Google (or your favorite
search engine).
There’s no ‘right way’ to do this so experiment and �nd what
works for you and the audience you’re researching. It’s worth the
effort to �nd Reddit discussions and forums — they’re often
incredible sources of deep insights about audience needs and
desires.
We’ve found that searching for AMAs (Ask Me Anything) is a
particularly good way to mine for themes and insights. Below is
an AMA by Aaron Sorkin (Reddit link here), the writer of The West
Wing. There are over 3,000 comments of budding authors and
screenwriters asking a master of his craft questions. Stop and
consider how valuable that is, especially when the expert was
interacting with his fans.
Reddit has an entire subreddits for AMAs, another rabbit hole of
audience insights to mine for gold.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 15 --

Once you’ve found a few threads / forums, immerse yourself in
those for an hour or two pulling on threads and see where they
lead. Again, we’re looking for audience insights — notice (and
capture in your notes) when you discover recurring themes.
Twitter
Twitter is an amazing research tool (and, like other social media,
it’s also a potential time waster so be careful).
We recommend that you start broadly using Twitter’s search
function, and then use Twitter Lists to curate your collection of
Twitter accounts to follow.
▪ Buffer has a good article on getting the most from Twitter
Lists: 23 Seldom-Used Ideas for How to Use Twitter Lists
▪ Zapier’s Ultimate Guide to Twitter Advanced Search is a
good place to start learning how to use Twitter for research.
Other
There are many other sources of audience information online.
However, we want to �nd the right balance between quality and
quantity of information and it’s very, very easy to become
overwhelmed with quantity.
Consider these as options that may apply to speci�c audiences —
if they’re relevant for your audience, that’s great. If not, ignore
them and move on.
▪ Medium reader comments. (Search articles by topic and
skim comments for useful information.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 15 --

▪ Instagram (content and comments).
▪ Kickstarter (comments and FAQs). Kickstarter (and similar
crowdfunding platforms) can be particularly useful for
physical products.
In-Person Summits & Masterminds
COVID aside, it can’t be overstated how valuable in-person niche
gatherings are at uncovering a world of intimate audience
insights. Nuances that only reveal themselves within the hubbub
of these closed events.
I (André) was invited to speak at the Smarter Artist Summit in
2016. I don’t do speaking engagements, but I begrudgingly
accepted as a favor to my three friends whose event this was. I’m
so glad I attended.
Below is my 3D point of view of the crowd who lobbed questions
like hand grenades during the AMA session. It was a room full of
authors of varying caliber, from wannabes to New York Times #1
bestsellers.
The most surprising theme that showed up was that the broad
majority of authors, regardless of their success and expertise, are
petri�ed to death of using email as a channel to sell their books.
This theme was validated repeatedly during the two days as I
spoke to people over coffee at lunch and beers at dinner.
I think some insights like this can be challenging to parse out on
online public forums and groups. Yet, at events like this, people
feel safe, so they share openly and vulnerably.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 15 --

Smarter Artist Summit, Austin (April 2016)
Smarter Artist Summit, Austin (April 2016)
The single word to describe this level of interaction is immersion.
Be curious, ask questions.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 15 --

The other insight I discovered was, no matter how experienced an
author was, they had pains they were struggling with, and gains
they were seeking to move towards. No author was immune to
having signi�cant �aws and issues that kept them up at night.
(This is a universal truth across any audience segment.)
Broadly speaking, online forums and groups aren’t populated by
experts in their craft. You won’t �nd Stephen King in a Facebook
Group lol’ing with fans. But events like this are attended by
experts within their category of expertise, and they’re almost
always accessible.
After the two-day main event, I attended a high-level mastermind.
There were perhaps seventeen six and seven-�gure authors in the
room. It was so interesting to see how similar the experts were to
the “ordinary” authors. They used the same services (email, book
funnels, publishing platforms, tools, methods of promotion, etc.).
The only difference was in the nuances and the context. But even
then, many of their pains and gains were thematically similar to
what everyone else was struggling with.
Compilation
Use these platforms to �nd where your minimum viable audience
is spending time and discover what matters to them, the problems
they care enough about to try and solve, etc.
The above list certainly isn’t exhaustive, but it’ll give you a great
head start and traction. See where the journey of discovery and
deep dive �y-on-the-wall observation takes you.
Allow it to take you wherever it takes you.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 15 --

On a meta-level you’re looking for social communities, wide and
narrow, where people are hanging out and connecting with each
other.
Overwhelm is a very serious issue, especially when you’re �rst
getting started. We recommend that you use time-blocking to
minimize overwhelm and maintain momentum and focus.
For example, spend one hour per day researching your audience
�ve days a week (or whatever is right for you). Use the time
constraint as a forcing mechanism, and approach the process like
a treasure hunt.
You’re looking for the highest value places your audience
congregates, and then you’re mining those places for the highest-
value insights you can �nd.
Capture those insights simply at �rst — Google docs, Word, or a
more sophisticated tool like Evernote will work. (We’ll
recommend more advanced tools later — for now, we don’t want
any barriers to progress.)
Don’t worry about trying to capture these insights exactly the
‘right way’ — just capture them. Start making lists, and you’ll
begin to sense what’s important because you’ll see it more often.
Patterns will emerge when you immerse yourself in the process.
In Part III we’ll discuss how to engage with individuals directly
for more nuanced feedback (and to �ll in any details that your
research has not yet revealed).
Action Items
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 15 --

1 — Identify 2-3 resources from the options above to mine for
audience insights.
2 — Establish time constraints one week at a time. For example, in
week one commit to 45 minutes of audience research per day and
stay committed to that constraint. (Don’t spend more or less time.)
3 — Create a simple document to begin capturing insights. Google
Docs, Word, Evernote, etc. — do not obsess about using the ‘right
tool’ or the ‘optimal method’. All you need at this stage is a digital
container to capture insights that seem interesting.
4 — Focus on one resource per day and immerse yourself in it.
Keep pulling on threads, and be willing to abandon any resources
that aren’t valuable.
5 — Whenever you �nd something interesting, copy it into your
document. Categorize very lightly organically — when you see the
same themes appearing group those comments together in your
document. Don’t try to force a structure — let that emerge over
time.
Use the private accountability community to have discussions
with your peers and share your insights and your work.
For questions, please use the comments below. (Once again: please
don’t show us your work for critique, we can’t possibly offer
individual feedback for everyone. Most importantly, this is work
you need to do to extract insights that will be meaningful only to
you deep in the trenches; a level of nuance that will be invisible to
us offering any level of critique.)
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

www.getwsodo.com
